labels.name: Select
labels.desc: Specifying which columns to retrieve from the database

labels.name: Filter
labels.desc: A condition that limits which rows are included
labels.example:
SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '90 days';

labels.name: Aggregate
labels.desc: A calculation across multiple rows — COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX

labels.name: Having
labels.desc: A condition that filters grouped results after aggregation
labels.example:
SELECT category, COUNT(*) AS total
FROM products
GROUP BY category
HAVING COUNT(*) > 5;

labels.name: Join
labels.desc: Combining rows from two related tables using a shared key

labels.name: Sort
labels.desc: Controlling the order of returned rows with ORDER BY

labels.name: Modify
labels.desc: Adding new rows or changing existing data with INSERT or UPDATE
labels.example:
INSERT INTO customers (name, email) VALUES ('Alice', 'alice@example.com');
UPDATE products SET price = 19.99 WHERE id = 42;

prompts.title: Frequent Customers
prompts.statement: The marketing team wants to target customers who placed at least three orders in the last 90 days. Write a query to retrieve their names and email addresses.

expectedTags.explanation: Counting orders per customer and keeping only those with three or more requires both grouping (to count per customer) and a post-aggregation condition to exclude customers below the threshold.
This date condition limits which orders are counted — only those placed within the last 90 days qualify.

expectedTags.phrase: retrieve their names and email addresses
expectedTags.explanation: Returning customer columns from the query requires selecting them explicitly, and because the order data lives in a separate table, the two tables must be joined to bring them together.

codeSnippets.join:
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id

prompts.code:
SELECT c.name, c.email
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id
WHERE o.order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '90 days'
GROUP BY c.id, c.name, c.email
HAVING COUNT(o.id) >= 3;

prompts.title: Category Summary
prompts.statement: A product manager wants a breakdown of each category: its name, how many products it contains, and the average price — but only for categories with more than five products.

expectedTags.explanation: Producing a per-category breakdown means grouping rows by category and applying aggregate functions — each group collapses into one summary row in the results.

codeSnippets.aggregate:
COUNT(*) AS product_count,
AVG(price) AS avg_price
GROUP BY category_name
codeSnippets.select: SELECT category_name, COUNT(*) AS product_count, AVG(price) AS avg_price

expectedTags.phrase: how many products it contains, and the average price
expectedTags.explanation: Counting products and computing an average price are both aggregate calculations — they collapse multiple rows into a single summary value per group.

codeSnippets.aggregate:
COUNT(*) AS product_count,
AVG(price) AS avg_price

expectedTags.phrase: only for categories with more than five products
expectedTags.explanation: Restricting results based on a count requires filtering after aggregation. WHERE cannot reference aggregate results, so HAVING is used instead.

prompts.code:
SELECT category_name,
       COUNT(*) AS product_count,
       AVG(price) AS avg_price
FROM products
GROUP BY category_name
HAVING COUNT(*) > 5
ORDER BY avg_price DESC;

prompts.title: Order Lookup by Customer
prompts.statement: Customer service needs to look up orders by customer name. Write a query that returns each matching order's ID, date, and total for the customer named 'Maria Santos'.

expectedTags.phrase: look up orders by customer name
expectedTags.explanation: Order and customer data live in separate tables, so a join is needed to link them. The customer name then acts as the condition that limits which rows are returned.

codeSnippets.join:
FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id

expectedTags.explanation: Naming the columns to return is the SELECT clause — it determines exactly which fields appear in the result.

prompts.code:
SELECT o.id, o.order_date, o.total
FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id
WHERE c.name = 'Maria Santos'
ORDER BY o.order_date DESC;

prompts.title: Employee–Manager Report
prompts.statement: HR wants to see every employee listed alongside their manager's name. Employees who don't have a manager assigned should still appear in the results.

expectedTags.phrase: every employee listed alongside their manager's name
expectedTags.explanation: Pairing each employee with their manager's name means joining the employees table with itself — one copy represents employees, the other represents managers.

codeSnippets.join:
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN employees m ON m.id = e.manager_id
codeSnippets.select: SELECT e.name AS employee, m.name AS manager

expectedTags.phrase: don't have a manager assigned should still appear
expectedTags.explanation: A LEFT JOIN keeps every row from the left table even when no match exists on the right — employees without a manager get a NULL in the manager column rather than being dropped.

codeSnippets.join: LEFT JOIN employees m ON m.id = e.manager_id

prompts.code:
SELECT e.name AS employee, m.name AS manager
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN employees m ON m.id = e.manager_id
ORDER BY e.name;

prompts.title: Customers Without Orders
prompts.statement: The sales team wants to identify customers who have never placed an order, so they can reach out to them. Write a query to find those customers.

expectedTags.explanation: Finding customers with no matching orders means joining the tables and then filtering for the unmatched side — rows where the join produced no order record.

codeSnippets.join:
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id

expectedTags.explanation: Naming the columns to return — here the customer name and email — is the SELECT clause.

prompts.code:
SELECT c.name, c.email
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id
WHERE o.id IS NULL
ORDER BY c.name;

prompts.title: Sales Leaderboard
prompts.statement: Sales leadership wants to see each salesperson's total sales for the current year, ranked from highest to lowest.

expectedTags.explanation: Summing sales per person requires grouping order totals by salesperson, which is an aggregation. The salesperson names live in a separate table, so a join is needed to bring them in.

codeSnippets.aggregate:
SUM(o.total) AS total_sales
GROUP BY e.id, e.name
codeSnippets.join:
FROM employees e
JOIN orders o ON o.salesperson_id = e.id

expectedTags.explanation: Restricting totals to orders from the current year is a date-based filter applied before grouping.

codeSnippets.filter: WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM o.order_date) = EXTRACT(YEAR FROM CURRENT_DATE)

expectedTags.explanation: Ordering results from largest to smallest total is controlled by ORDER BY with the DESC direction.

prompts.code:
SELECT e.name, SUM(o.total) AS total_sales
FROM employees e
JOIN orders o ON o.salesperson_id = e.id
WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM o.order_date) = EXTRACT(YEAR FROM CURRENT_DATE)
GROUP BY e.id, e.name
ORDER BY total_sales DESC;

prompts.title: Safe Price Update
prompts.statement: A product manager needs to change the price of product ID 7 to $24.99. Write a SQL statement to update that record.

expectedTags.explanation: Changing an existing value in a row is an UPDATE — it targets a specific column and sets a new value without replacing the whole row.
The WHERE clause identifies exactly which row to update — without it, every row in the table would be changed.

prompts.title: Department Headcount
prompts.statement: HR wants a report showing how many employees are in each department, listed alphabetically by department name.

expectedTags.phrase: how many employees are in each department
expectedTags.explanation: Counting employees per department means grouping all rows by department and applying COUNT — each group collapses into one summary row with the department's total.

codeSnippets.aggregate:
COUNT(*) AS headcount
GROUP BY department

expectedTags.explanation: Ordering results alphabetically is controlled by ORDER BY on the department column.

prompts.code:
SELECT department, COUNT(*) AS headcount
FROM employees
GROUP BY department
ORDER BY department ASC;
